question: Define a logical disjunction (OR operation) and construct its complete truth table. Explain the difference between its meaning in everyday language and its meaning in formal logic.
expectedPoints: Definition: A disjunction p ∨ q is false only when both p and q are false; otherwise, it is true.
Truth Table: Columns for p, q, p∨q with the following rows: (T,T)=T; (T,F)=T; (F,T)=T; (F,F)=F.
Key Difference: In everyday language, 'or' is often exclusive (like 'either...or'). In formal logic, the disjunction is inclusive, meaning 'and/or'.

question: Using the truth tables for implication (→) and conjunction (∧), prove the equivalence ¬(p → q) ≡ p ∧ ¬q. Explain the logical significance of this equivalence.
expectedPoints: Construct the truth table for p → q, which is false only when p is true and q is false.
Construct the truth table for ¬(p → q), which is true only when p is true and q is false.
Construct the truth table for p ∧ ¬q, which is also true only when p is true and q is false.
Conclusion: Since their truth values match for all combinations of p and q, the statements are logically equivalent.
Significance: This shows that denying an implication is not the same as denying its conclusion; it is asserting that the hypothesis is true and the conclusion is false.

question:
A new binary connective, denoted by '◊', is defined by the following truth table. Determine if the set of connectives {◊, ¬} is functionally complete. Justify your answer rigorously.

| p | q | p ◊ q |
|---|---|-------|
| T | T |   F   |
| T | F |   T   |
| F | T |   T   |
| F | F |   F   |
expectedPoints: Identify the Connective: The truth table shows that p ◊ q is true only when exactly one of p or q is true. This is the definition of the exclusive OR (XOR) operation.
Functional Completeness Definition: A set of connectives is functionally complete if every possible truth function can be expressed using only connectives from that set.

Express Known Connectives: Show that the negation (¬) and conjunction (∧) or another known complete set can be built from {◊, ¬}. For example, prove that p ∧ q ≡ ¬(p ◊ q) ◊ (p ◊ ¬q) or find another combination.
Alternative Proof: Note that {¬, ∧} is functionally complete. Since we have ¬, we only need to express ∧ using ◊ and ¬. p ∧ q can be expressed as ¬(p ◊ ¬q) (this can be verified with a truth table).
Conclusion: Since both negation and conjunction can be expressed, the set {◊, ¬} is functionally complete.
